response:
## Decision: Run the full pre-registered grid (reject pilot-first/early-stop)

**Call:** When Issue #41's tiering test executes, it runs the full 4-arm × 8-task × 3-rep pre-registered design (ISSUE-41.md:33–43, 68). No arm-level early stop on a partial grid. The only sanctioned in-flight adaptation is the one already pre-registered at line 49 — widening reps/tasks when a CI straddles the decision boundary — never truncating arms.

### Rationale

1. **Early stopping is definitionally the fishing pattern the pre-reg exists to prevent.** Line 25 states the pre-reg's purpose is to avoid "fishing for a favorable cut." The document pre-registers exactly one sequential-adaptation rule (line 49), and it's a *widening* rule (add data on ambiguity), not a truncation rule (stop on an early favorable read). "Pilot the expensive arm, stop when decidable" is an unregistered stopping rule invented after the fact — indistinguishable from optional stopping under audit, which would retroactively void the ADR 0024 cost-justification loop this test feeds.

2. **The task-gradient pre-screen (line 42) has to run against the full 8-task set before any arm is decisive.** Line 54 is explicit the test is uninformative without the Haiku<Opus gradient. A pilot of the single expensive arm against a subset of tasks can look decidable while running on tasks that turn out saturated once the rest of the grid is ever completed — and a stopped-early design never surfaces that contamination.

3. **The assumption probe's own load-bearing premise for Option 2 doesn't hold here.** The decision rule (line 51) is *relative* to Sonnet-solo ("delta vs Sonnet-solo >= -0.05 AND tokens <= 0.7x Sonnet-solo"), so an expensive-arm pilot is only decidable if a valid Sonnet-solo baseline for these exact 8 tasks already exists to compare against. The issue gives no indication one does — all 4 arms are specified to run on "the same tasks" (line 33), implying baseline generation is part of this test, not reuse of prior data. Absent that pre-existing baseline, Option 2 structurally collapses back into Option 1 anyway — piloting the expensive arm alone can't produce a decidable gate.

### Assumptions and risks (stated, not buried in an acceptance criterion)

- **Load-bearing assumption (reverses the decision if false):** No valid Sonnet-solo baseline for this exact 8-task set already exists from prior benchmark work — so generating it as part of the full grid is necessary spend, not redundant spend. If a usable Sonnet-solo baseline for these tasks *does* already exist, the marginal cost of Option 1 is inflated by a redundant baseline run, and a cheaper pilot-first design becomes admissible without reintroducing the fishing problem.
- **Failure class accepted:** paying the full ~250–300 invocation cost (line 71) even in the world where the gate turns out to be unambiguously decidable early — i.e., accepting redundant spend as the price of preserving pre-registration integrity, rather than accepting the fishing/task-contamination/undersampled-validator-fidelity failure class that an early-stopped pilot (premortem findings 1–3) would risk.
- Secondary risk carried forward, not eliminated by this call: the validator-fidelity diagnostic (line 56) still needs to be checked for adequate sample size within the full grid's rep count — the full grid makes this measurable, it doesn't guarantee it's sufficient, and that's a separate check to run at analysis time, not a re-litigation of this call.

This is a judgment call under the repo's PDCA rule — recording it here per the pre-reg's own accept-and-record-either-way stance (line 51); it does not require a separate ADR file since it operationalizes an already-registered test plan rather than setting new policy.
